description: Goodfire is an AI research lab using interpretability to turn AI into something that can be understood, debugged, and shaped like software

build_patterns.name: Micro-model Meshes
evidence: hierarchical attention transformer to predict AD. The first level ... aggregates Pleiades embeddings from cfDNA fragments into a single embedding per DMR. The second level aggregates DMR embeddings into a single patient-level embedding
we train lightweight probes to test if specific biological signals can be extracted from Pleiades embeddings
Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) ... decompose the model's representations and trace which components drive the disease classification
we distilled this insight into a human-interpretable classifier ... a simple proxy model using only fragment length features
build_patterns.description: Multiple specialized models and components are used together rather than a single monolith: a large foundation model (Pleiades) provides embeddings, supervised lightweight probes and BatchTopK SAEs are used for interpretability, a hierarchical attention transformer aggregates at fragment→DMR→patient levels, and a distilled logistic-regression proxy is used for deployment/validation.

build_patterns.name: Guardrail-as-LLM
evidence: Deploying Interpretability to Production with Rakuten: SAE Probes for PII Detection
one of the first uses of sparse autoencoders (SAEs) with a production AI model - using SAE probes to detect personally identifiable information for Rakuten AI agents
probes are far more efficient and inexpensive on the task than LLM-as-judge baselines
build_patterns.description: Secondary specialized models (SAE probes) are used as a safety/compliance layer to detect PII in agent inputs, functioning like a guardrail that checks or filters content produced or ingested by other systems; explicitly contrasted with LLM-as-judge approaches.

build_patterns.name: Vertical Data Moats
evidence: The model was pretrained on 1.9 trillion tokens from this combined dataset
Data was sourced from genomic and cell-free DNA across multiple cell types and sequenced with methods that preserve cytosine methylation state
Oxford University study with over 30 billion cfDNA fragments across a cohort of 81 individuals
build_patterns.description: Heavy reliance on large, domain-specific, proprietary/curated biological datasets (cfDNA, methylation, DMR-focused fragments) that provide a specialized competitive advantage for epigenetic and clinical tasks.

build_patterns.name: Continuous-learning Flywheels
evidence: We hope this offers a case study of how we can use interpretability to surface candidate signals, build simple models to test them, and let the results inform the next round of experiments - both in the lab and in silico
validation in larger cohorts underway
build_patterns.description: There are hints of iterative experimentation and planned validation loops (interpretable hypotheses → proxy models → follow-up experiments), but no explicit production feedback pipelines, automated user-feedback ingestion, or model updating loop described.

build_patterns.name: RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation)
build_patterns.description: No evidence of retrieval components, vector stores, document search, or retrieval integrated into generation; the system operates on sequence embeddings and hierarchical aggregation rather than retrieval-augmented text generation.

build_patterns.name: Agentic Architectures
build_patterns.description: No autonomous multi-step agents, tool use, or orchestration of chains of actions are described; tasks are analytic, interpretability, and classification-focused.

build_patterns.name: Natural-Language-to-Code
build_patterns.description: No mention of translating natural language into executable code or rule generation from text.

build_patterns.name: Knowledge Graphs
build_patterns.description: No evidence of graph-based knowledge representations, RBAC/permission graphs, or graph databases; the work focuses on sequence embeddings, DMRs, and fragment-level features.

discovered_patterns.pattern_name: Domain-specific epigenetic foundation model with methylation-aware tokenization
evidence: "Pleiades is a 7-billion parameter epigenetic foundation model trained on human methylation and genomic sequences."
"Tokens represented either individual nucleotides or the presence of a methyl group."
"The model was pretrained on 1.9 trillion tokens from this combined dataset."
"Training proceeded in two stages: pretraining on next-token prediction, followed by additional training on a reconstruction task."
discovered_patterns.description: They built a foundation model specialized for epigenomics: bespoke tokenization that encodes both nucleotide identity and methylation presence, very large pretraining corpus (1.9T tokens) composed of genomic and cfDNA sequences, and two complementary pretraining objectives (next-token and reconstruction) to learn higher-order epigenetic representations.
discovered_patterns.why_notable: Tokenizing methylation as first-class tokens and pretraining a mid-size (~7B) foundation model specifically on genomic + cfDNA data at trillion-token scale is rare and purpose-built for biological representation learning, enabling embedding-level downstream uses rather than just sequence prediction.

discovered_patterns.pattern_name: Hierarchical attention transformer over fragment embeddings
evidence: "To combine information across fragments, the Prima Mente team used a hierarchical attention transformer to predict AD."

"The first level of the hierarchical classifier aggregates Pleiades embeddings from cfDNA fragments into a single embedding per DMR. The second level aggregates DMR embeddings into a single patient-level embedding which is then used for the final classification."
discovered_patterns.description: Downstream classifier is a two-level hierarchical attention transformer: fragment embeddings (from Pleiades) -> aggregated per DMR -> aggregated per patient -> classification. This explicitly encodes the biological hierarchy (fragments → DMRs → patient) into the model architecture.
discovered_patterns.why_notable: Explicit architectural alignment to biological hierarchy (fragments→DMR→patient) is an effective structural inductive bias for molecular diagnostic tasks and is more structured than flat pooling or simple aggregation of embeddings.

discovered_patterns.pattern_name: Use of open/known LLM as sidecar (Llama 3.1 8B) for activation harvesting
evidence: "We trained an SAE on a middle layer of Llama 3.1 8B on a mixed-language dataset to learn a wide distribution of useful features..."

"The activations are generated by a sidecar model, a smaller and faster model that runs in parallel with the primary model producing user-facing responses. In our configuration, we employ Llama 3.1 8B as the sidecar."
discovered_patterns.description: For production PII detection the team runs a lighter, open-model (Llama 3.1 8B) in tandem with the main agent; they harvest intermediate activations from a middle layer and train SAEs/probes on those activations rather than on raw text.
discovered_patterns.why_notable: Using a sidecar model for activation probing is a pragmatic/efficient pattern but relies on well-known models; novelty is modest but it's a concrete, operationalized deployment choice.

discovered_patterns.pattern_name: Sidecar probing + main agent separation (activation-based monitoring)
evidence: "The activations are generated by a sidecar model, a smaller and faster model that runs in parallel with the primary model producing user-facing responses."
"Probing a sidecar model yields dramatically better performance (96% vs. 51%) than using the same model as a black-box judge."
discovered_patterns.description: They decouple monitoring/probing from the primary conversational model: a small sidecar model runs in parallel, providing activations for SAEs and probes. This enables efficient, high-recall detection (e.g., PII) without routing every input to a heavyweight black-box judge or sending raw inputs into other services.
discovered_patterns.why_notable: Practical orchestration pattern that balances cost, latency, and privacy. The explicit use of a sidecar for activation-based monitors is uncommon in published production descriptions and demonstrates system-level compound AI orchestration.

discovered_patterns.pattern_name: Atlas-driven cell-type deconvolution as external knowledge
evidence: "We implement cell type deconvolution with a methylation-based approach, comparing observed DMR methylation rates to previously reported methylation rates across a cell type atlas [13, 14]."
discovered_patterns.description: Instead of RAG, they leverage published domain atlases (methylation atlas and single-cell methylation maps) to compute deconvolution features—comparing observed fragment methylation to known cell-type methylation signatures to estimate tissue-of-origin contributions.
discovered_patterns.why_notable: Using domain atlases as structured external knowledge for feature derivation is standard in genomics but notable here because it's combined with foundation-model embeddings and fragmentomics features.

discovered_patterns.pattern_name: Interpretability-led eval pipeline: probes, SAEs, gradient attribution and manifold analysis
evidence: "For our supervised approach, we train lightweight probes to test if specific biological signals can be extracted from Pleiades embeddings for single DNA fragments."
"We then use gradient attribution to identify nine SAE features responsible for the majority of disease classification performance."
"We project Pleiades fragment embeddings into a 3D PCA space and observe a well-defined manifold... measure local curvature to identify regions that are encoded with different precision than others."
discovered_patterns.description: They built a layered interpretability evaluation: supervised probing (lightweight probes), unsupervised disentangling via sparse autoencoders (SAE), attribution of SAE features to downstream task, and embedding manifold analysis (PCA + curvature) to localize privileged fragment lengths—then validate by distillation and cohort testing.
discovered_patterns.why_notable: This is a deep, methodical pipeline that moves from representation probing → unsupervised concept discovery → attribution → geometric manifold inspection → biological hypothesis → distilled model testing. That full chain is uncommon and shows interpretability driving experimental design.

discovered_patterns.pattern_name: Probes and SAEs as lightweight production guardrails with cost-and-latency rationale
evidence: "probes are far more efficient and inexpensive on the task than LLM-as-judge baselines"
"probes offer 10–500x cost savings compared to common LLM-as-judge setups with comparable accuracy on the PII detection task"
"SAE probes outperformed other approaches when generalizing from synthetic to real production data"
"Rakuten deployed the SAE probes - the first known enterprise application of SAEs for language model guardrails."
discovered_patterns.description: They operationalize SAE probes in production (Rakuten): training SAEs on sidecar activations, freezing SAEs, training lightweight classifiers on SAE activations, and deploying these classifiers as high-recall guardrails. The justification emphasizes cost, throughput, and generalization from synthetic data.
discovered_patterns.why_notable: Enterprise deployment of SAEs for PII detection (with quantified cost savings) is a concrete LLMOps pattern that others can replicate; it's an early productionization of interpretability artifacts.

discovered_patterns.pattern_name: Synthetic-only training with high-recall deployment requirement and sidecar privacy posture
evidence: "Due to the sensitive nature of real PII, the training dataset was entirely synthetic."
"the methods needed to be lightweight, since they would be used on all interactions with the agent platform, and required very high recall - avoiding false negatives was paramount."
"This means that our methods' performance would need to transfer from a synthetic training dataset to real production data, a substantial distribution shift"
discovered_patterns.description: For PII detection they trained probes only on synthetic labeled data to avoid exposing real PII, prioritized recall in design, and used sidecar activation probing to reduce the need to transmit raw inputs for external judging—minimizing data exposure and aligning to privacy constraints.
discovered_patterns.why_notable: Combining synthetic-only training, activation-based detection via sidecars, and SAE generalization to real data addresses a practical enterprise privacy constraint that many teams face but rarely publish solutions for.

discovered_patterns.pattern_name: Interpretability-driven hypothesis generation and distillation into simple models
evidence: "Our interpretability tools identified the prominence of fragment length in Pleiades, guiding us towards its prioritization..."
"We distilled this insight into a human-interpretable classifier that generalizes better than the biomarker classes previously reported..."

"we focused on seven fragment length metrics computed across 354 DMRs... rank-normalized across all DMRs within each individual... feature selection over 100 bootstraps... Features selected in at least 90% of bootstraps were retained"
discovered_patterns.description: They used interpretability outputs (SAE features, probes, PCA manifold) to prioritize fragmentomic features, then distilled those insights into a logistic regression with careful feature engineering, rank-normalization, and bootstrap-based robust feature selection to validate generalization on independent cohorts.
discovered_patterns.why_notable: Using interpretability outputs as the direct mechanism for hypothesis triage and then validating via distilled, human-interpretable models (with robust statistical feature selection) is an explicit, well-executed pipeline linking model internals to experimental science.

discovered_patterns.pattern_name: Large, curated multi-modal pretraining corpus and batch-effect mitigation
evidence: "Data was sourced from genomic and cell-free DNA across multiple cell types and sequenced with methods that preserve cytosine methylation state."
"The model was pretrained on 1.9 trillion tokens from this combined dataset."
"To mitigate batch effects, each feature type was rank-normalized across all DMRs within each individual, converting absolute measurements into relative rankings."
discovered_patterns.description: They assembled a domain-specific, large-scale pretraining corpus mixing genomic DNA and cfDNA (with methylation-preserving sequencing) and apply straightforward statistical normalizations (rank-normalization per individual) to mitigate batch effects when building downstream features.
discovered_patterns.why_notable: 1.9T token pretraining on methylation-tokenized genome+cfDNA is a significant data moat; explicit per-individual rank-normalization to mitigate inter-batch variability is a practical pattern for small clinical cohorts.

novel_approaches.why_novel: They systematically convert interpretability artifacts (probes, SAEs, gradient attributions, embedding manifold geometry) into concrete biological hypotheses (fragment length biomarkers), then distill those into simple, validated models (logistic regression) that generalize to independent cohorts.
novel_approaches.potential_impact: Creates a reusable workflow for turning model internals into actionable, human-interpretable biomarkers or features—bridging black-box models and experimental biology, accelerating hypothesis triage and validation.

novel_approaches.approach: Production SAE probes on sidecar activations for PII detection
novel_approaches.why_novel: Enterprise deployment of SAEs as monitoring/guardrail components using a sidecar model, trained on synthetic data and demonstrating robust transfer to real production data, is an uncommon, operationalized use of interpretability artifacts.
novel_approaches.potential_impact: Enables low-cost, high-recall privacy/security controls compatible with strict data governance, reducing reliance on expensive LLM-as-judge paradigms and avoiding exposure of raw user data.

novel_approaches.approach: Methylation-aware tokenization in a genomic foundation model
novel_approaches.why_novel: Treating DNA bases and methylation presence as distinct tokens in pretraining and scaling to 1.9T tokens is a domain-specific tokenization enabling the model to jointly represent sequence and epigenetic state.
novel_approaches.potential_impact: Allows foundation models to capture epigenetic phenomena (e.g., fragmentomics, nucleosome footprints) in their latent space, enabling downstream biological discovery and clinically-relevant tasks from embeddings rather than requiring bespoke handcrafted features.

primary_models: Llama 3.1 8B (sidecar for activation harvesting and SAE training)
Hierarchical attention transformer (downstream classifier aggregating Pleiades embeddings)

fine_tuning.fine_tuning_approach: Pleiades: two-stage pretraining (next-token prediction then reconstruction). Downstream: classifiers trained on frozen Pleiades embeddings (hierarchical attention transformer). SAEs trained on model activations (unsupervised). Lightweight probes/classifiers trained on SAE activations (supervised). No explicit mention of LoRA or full fine-tune of Pleiades.
fine_tuning.training_data_source: Pleiades pretraining used genomic and cfDNA sequences (1.9T tokens) preserving methylation. Downstream classifiers used cfDNA fragments from an Oxford cohort (81 individuals) and an independent OPTIMA cohort (25 individuals). SAE for PII detection trained on synthetic PII dataset; SAE activations trained on Llama 3.1 middle-layer activations.

inference_optimization: Sidecar model pattern to reduce cost and latency (smaller Llama 3.1 8B running in parallel)
Use of lightweight probes and frozen SAEs to avoid repeated heavy inference
Design motivated by cost comparisons versus LLM-as-judge (10–500x savings claimed)

model_routing.routing_strategy: Implicit routing: sidecar model runs in parallel to the primary agent for monitoring/probing tasks; hierarchical transformer aggregates fragment→DMR→patient embeddings rather than routing between multiple specialist models. No evidence of dynamic MoE or task-based model routing.

compound_ai.orchestration_pattern: Composite system: Pleiades acts as foundation representation provider; a hierarchical attention transformer consumes embeddings for clinical predictions. Separately, production agent stack uses a primary conversational model plus a sidecar (Llama 3.1 8B) for activation-based probing; SAEs and probes run as sidecar analytics and guardrails. Orchestration is parallel/sidecar rather than sequential chain-of-thought handoffs.

sub_vertical: biomarker discovery for neurodegenerative diseases (diagnostic tests)
sub_sub_vertical: blood-based cfDNA fragmentomics for Alzheimer's disease detection

signals: Public research blog posts detailing interpretability workflows and hypothesis-driven analysis (transparent methodology).
Production-oriented blog post about using sparse autoencoders to detect PII in production environments (Rakuten collaboration).
Case studies and preprint links indicating engagement with real-world datasets and performance reporting across cohorts.
Explicit discussion of model interpretability, probes, and feature-level analysis suggesting engineering-minded reproducibility practices.

founders.name: Nicholas Wang
founders.role: Founder / CEO (inferred) of Goodfire Research; corresponding author
founders.background: Not explicitly stated in provided content
founders.domain_expertise: AI interpretability, biomarker discovery from cfDNA and epigenetics

team_analysis.founder_market_fit: Founders appear to have access to AI and biomedical data with cross-institution collaboration, aligning with disease biomarker discovery; explicit founder backgrounds are limited in the provided content, yielding a moderate assessment.
team_strengths: Interpretability-driven hypothesis generation, employing supervised probing and unsupervised sparse autoencoders to identify driving features in model decisions.
Strong domain integration of fragmentomics and methylation signals for Alzheimer's detection, leveraging multi-modal biological signals.
Cross-institution collaboration among Goodfire Research, University of Oxford, and Prima Mente, enabling access to diverse data sources and clinical context.
Ability to distill complex foundation-model insights into simple, testable proxy models (e.g., fragment-length based logistic regression) with cross-cohort generalization.
team_red_flags: Small cohort size and limited demographic diversity; generalizability to broader populations remains to be validated.
Interpretability-to-biology gap; results require experimental validation to establish causal biological mechanisms.
Lack of explicit hiring or team-structure signals in the provided content.

pricing_evidence: No pricing or pricing model discussed in content.

evidence: Public blog post detailing interpretability-driven biomarker discovery; case study style; references to independent cohorts; publication-like content.

revenue_model.monetization_approach: Enterprise software/licensing and services for downstream biomarker discovery and disease-detection analytics, leveraging Pleiades embeddings and interpretability platform; collaboration-driven revenue from biotech/pharma clients.
unit_economics_signals: No explicit margins or ARPU; early-stage proof-of-concept with small cohorts; potential for licensing and services revenue.

distribution_advantages: Academic collaborations (Oxford) and credibility from Goodfire Research; large-scale foundation model deployment; potential data/network effects from embeddings and DMRs across cohorts.

customer_proof_points: AUROC results (0.84 with mix of features)

stage_evidence: pilot study with small sample size (81 Oxford cohort; 25 in independent OPTIMA cohort)
no public API, SDKs, pricing, or deployment details described
explicit note that validation across larger, more diverse populations is needed
early results with larger cohort validation underway

core_features: Pleiades: a 7B epigenetic foundation model trained on human methylation and genomic sequences
downstream classifier leveraging Pleiades embeddings to detect Alzheimer's disease from cfDNA
interpretable analysis tools (supervised probing, sparse autoencoders, gradient attribution) to identify dominant signals
proxy model distilled from fragment length features for an interpretable, low-complexity predictor
ability to integrate fragmentomics with methylation signals in a hierarchical framework
differentiating_features: fragment length as the dominant predictive signal, contrasting expectations that methylation would be primary
interpretability-driven discovery of fragmentomics as a novel biomarker class for AD
capacity to distill complex model representations into a simple logistic regression model with reasonable cross-cohort generalization
roadmap_signals: validation across larger and more diverse populations
experimental validation of proposed biological mechanisms beyond interpretability
continued exploration of fragmentomic features and their combination with methylation and cell-type signals
extension to other diseases and broader diagnostic targets via interpretability-guided hypotheses

integrations_mentioned: downstream classifier built on Pleiades embeddings
collaboration with Prima Mente for downstream applications
utilization of Oxford OPTIMA cohort data for validation

use_cases.primary_use_case: detect Alzheimer's disease from blood cfDNA using fragment length and methylation signals
secondary_use_cases: potential applicability to other neurodegenerative diseases (though not generalizable to Parkinson's in this study)
use of interpretability to generate testable biological hypotheses and drive experimental prioritization

product_risks: small, non-diverse cohorts limit generalizability and robustness across populations
interpretability signals may reflect data artifacts or confounders rather than true biology
need for prospective validation, replication, and regulatory considerations before deployment
limited information on deployment readiness, pricing, and scalable tooling
product_strengths: novel fragmentomics biomarker class for AD discovered via interpretability
dominant role of fragment length with plausible biological rationale tied to nucleosome footprints
ability to distill complex representations into a simple, generalizable proxy model
foundation-model approach enables rapid hypothesis generation and experimental prioritization

unique_findings: Hybrid tokenization that encodes both nucleotide identity and methylation presence as atomic tokens in a 7B-parameter foundation model trained on ~1.9 trillion tokens — this is an unusual, domain-native token scheme that forces the model to learn joint sequence + epigenetic representations rather than treating methylation as side metadata.

Two-stage pretraining objective: next-token prediction followed by a separate reconstruction task. That dual objective appears designed to produce both generative sequence modeling skill and robust denoised encodings useful for downstream clinical classifiers.

Hierarchical attention transformer for cfDNA: a two-level aggregator that first pools fragment embeddings into DMR-level embeddings, then pools DMRs into a patient embedding. This architecture explicitly mirrors biological hierarchy (fragment → region → patient) and lets them scale from millions of fragments to single-sample predictions.

Use of sparse autoencoders (BatchTopK SAE) trained on the model's pretraining distribution to decompose internal embeddings into sparse, human-interpretable 'features' (concepts), followed by gradient attribution on SAE components to identify which emergent concepts drive a downstream classifier. This is a relatively novel pipeline: use unsupervised disentangling on pretraining representations, then attribution to link components to task performance.

Empirical manifold analysis of fragment embeddings: projecting fragment-level embeddings into 3D PCA space and using local curvature to detect 'privileged' fragment lengths (notably minimal curvature at 167 bp and peaks at 147 bp). This is a creative use of geometric signal analysis to map continuous biological variables onto representation geometry.

Distillation of representation-derived hypotheses into a tiny, fully interpretable classifier: rank-normalizing fragment-length-derived metrics across DMRs per individual, bootstrapped feature selection (100 bootstraps, retention threshold ≥90%), and final logistic regression on 11 features out of 2478 candidates. This enforces robustness to batch effects and produces a human-inspectable model that generalizes.

Operational emphasis on fragmentomics over methylation and cell-type-deconvolution signals: probing showed methylation is accessible but fragment length dominates decision-making; fragmentomic-only model generalizes better to an independent cohort than methylation-only models — an unexpected domain insight surfaced by interpretability rather than hypothesis-first biology.

Practical anti-batch strategy: within-individual rank-normalization of DMR metrics (turning absolute fragment counts/metrics into per-sample relative ranks across DMRs). This is a simple but effective choice for cross-cohort robustness when absolute sequencing depths and wet-lab protocols vary.

Enterprise interpretability deployment: using SAEs as probes for PII detection in production (Rakuten), showing synthetic→real generalization and cost/compute advantages over LLM-as-judge baselines. This demonstrates the same interpretability primitives can be productized outside biology for compliance-sensitive tasks.

story_angles.headline: AI overturned the textbook: fragment length, not methylation, drove Alzheimer’s detection
story_angles.summary: Prima Mente and Goodfire used interpretability tools on a 7B epigenetic foundation model and found fragmentomic signals (cfDNA fragment lengths) dominated AD predictions—contradicting the field’s methylation-first assumptions. That insight was distilled into a simple logistic model that generalized better across cohorts than methylation-only classifiers.
evidence: Sparse autoencoders + gradient attribution identified nine SAE features responsible for most AD classification and these correlated strongly with fragment length.
Fragment-only logistic regression achieved AUROC=0.78 (95% CI [0.56–0.95]) on an independent cohort, while methylation-only models failed to generalize (AUROC≈0.55).

story_angles.headline: From tokenized methylation to hierarchy: how Pleiades and a hierarchical attention transformer decode cfDNA at scale
story_angles.summary: Pleiades is a methylation-aware 7B foundation model trained on 1.9 trillion tokens (bases + methylation) whose fragment-level embeddings are aggregated via a two-level hierarchical attention transformer over DMRs to produce patient-level AD predictions. The team paired probing, BatchTopK SAEs, manifold analysis, and simple distilled models to close the loop from internals to interpretable biomarkers.
evidence: Pleiades uses methylation-aware tokenization and was pretrained on 1.9 trillion tokens, then fine-tuned with a reconstruction objective.
Downstream classifier uses a hierarchical attention transformer: fragment embeddings → per-DMR embedding → patient embedding, enabling aggregation across millions of cfDNA fragments per sample.

story_angles.headline: The secret weapon: cfDNA fragmentomics and a trillion‑token epigenetic corpus
story_angles.summary: Beyond model architecture, the dataset itself gives Pleiades an edge: combined genomic and cfDNA sequencing that preserves cytosine methylation plus fragment length metadata at massive scale enabled the model to encode both methylation and fragmentomics. That multimodal pretraining unlocked signals inaccessible to prior methylation-only studies.
evidence: Training data combined genomic DNA and cfDNA (which includes fragment length profiles) and preserved cytosine methylation state across samples.

Pretraining scale: ~1.9 trillion tokens spanning sequences and methylation indicators, enabling the model to learn a fragment length manifold with biologically meaningful curvature (e.g., 147 bp and 167 bp).

story_angles.headline: Interpretability as hypothesis engine: turning model internals into testable neurodegeneration biomarkers
story_angles.summary: The team built an interpretability pipeline (probes, BatchTopK SAEs, gradient attribution, manifold PCA) to triage candidate biomarkers from a foundation model and then distilled those signals into a human-interpretable classifier tied back to DMRs and nearby genes—demonstrating a concrete workflow for AI-guided biomarker discovery in neurology.
evidence: Supervised probes showed Pleiades encodes fragment-level methylation and length; SAEs and attributions prioritized fragment length for disease prediction.

Selected fragmentomic DMR features aggregated to nearby genes and implicated both neural and immune cell-type signals, with the resulting model tested for disease specificity against Parkinson’s (AUROC≈0.51).

anti_patterns.description: Unclear sustainable competitive advantage — while the paper describes a large foundation model and a sophisticated interpretability pipeline, key elements that create a durable moat are not fully evident. The downstream result (a fragment-length–based classifier) is built on a small clinical cohort and could be re-derived by others given access to similar cfDNA data and standard fragmentomics pipelines.
evidence: Pleiades is a 7-billion parameter model pretrained on 1.9 trillion tokens from combined genomic and cfDNA datasets (claims scale).
The final proxy model is a logistic regression distilled to 11 features from 2478 candidates and trained/tested on 81 (training) + 25 (independent test) patients, a small cohort.

Authors acknowledge sample size, cohort diversity, and interpretability-to-biology gaps: 'This is an early proof-of-concept... based on 81 patients from a single Oxford University cohort... requires validation across larger, more diverse populations.'

Fragmentomics and methylation analyses are active areas of research with several recent publications — the feature set may be reproducible by other academic / commercial groups with access to cfDNA sequencing.

anti_patterns.description: Certain framing risks overstating practical readiness and biological certainty. The narrative situates interpretability as a direct route to hypothesis discovery and positions fragment length as a 'novel class of biomarkers' for AD despite small sample size, wide confidence intervals, and acknowledged confounding possibilities.
evidence: Headline: 'Using Interpretability to Identify a Novel Class of Alzheimer's Biomarkers' — suggests discovery rather than preliminary hypothesis.
Reported performance has wide confidence intervals: 'AUROC = 0.78, 95% CI [0.56–0.95]' and downstream mixed models with wide CIs (e.g. AUROC = 0.84, 95% CI [0.66, 0.97]).

Authors explicitly note the interpretability-to-biology gap: 'Interpretability tells us what features drive the model's predictions, not whether those features reflect underlying disease biology or artifacts of data collection.'

anti_patterns.description: The deliverable described is effectively a research-derived feature set / proxy classifier rather than a defined product offering. There is limited discussion of operationalization, deployment, regulatory pathway, or robustness to clinical confounders.
evidence: The distillation yields a logistic regression using 11 features derived from fragment length metrics across 354 DMRs — presented and validated as a proof-of-concept rather than a deployed assay.
Limitations focus on cohort size, interpretability-to-biology gap, and need for larger validation: 'The results presented here are on small datasets with validation in larger cohorts underway.'
No discussion of clinical validation steps, regulatory strategy, standardization of sample processing, or deployment pipelines.

anti_patterns.description: Risk of being undifferentiated in the broader cfDNA/biomarker market — fragmentomics and methylation-based cfDNA diagnostics are active, competitive areas. The incremental advantage beyond existing methylation/cell-type deconvolution approaches relies on validation and whether the Pleiades interpretability pipeline yields reproducibly unique signals.
evidence: The manuscript cites multiple recent high-profile fragmentomics and cfDNA methylation works (Nature Medicine 2025; Nature 2019; other 2023–2025 references), showing active competition in the same problem space.
The authors themselves expected methylation to dominate based on literature, and fragmentomics emerged unexpectedly — this could be novel but requires confirmation to be a durable differentiator.

competitors.name: Anthropic
competitors.similarity: Enterprise-grade work on model safety, interpretability research, and building LLMs designed for trustworthy behaviour.
competitors.how_different: Goodfire focuses on mechanistic interpretability (SAEs, probes) and operationalizes interpretability for domain-specific hypothesis generation (biology) and lightweight production probes; Anthropic builds general-purpose LLMs and safety tooling at scale rather than bespoke interpretability-to-science pipelines.

competitors.name: DeepMind / Google Research (interpretability teams)
competitors.similarity: Deep, academic-quality work on model internals, mechanistic interpretability, and publishing methods that investigate how models represent biological and other signals.
competitors.how_different: Goodfire packages interpretability as a product/practical workflow (SAE probes, sidecar probe deployment, hypothesis distillation into simple classifiers) and targets commercial applications (biomarker discovery, PII guards) with an emphasis on translating internals to testable biology.

competitors.name: Fiddler AI (and enterprise explainability/monitoring vendors)
competitors.similarity: Provide explainability, model monitoring and probes for enterprise ML governance and compliance.
competitors.how_different: Fiddler is focused on feature-/decision-level explainability and monitoring for tabular/ML pipelines; Goodfire emphasizes mechanistic decomposition of large model activations (SAEs), lightweight sidecar probes for real-time guardrails, and demonstrable cross-domain R&D use (epigenetics biomarker discovery).

competitors.name: GRAIL / Guardant Health / other cfDNA diagnostics (fragmentomics & methylation diagnostics)
competitors.similarity: Develop clinical cfDNA-based diagnostics using fragmentomics and methylation signatures to detect disease from blood.
competitors.how_different: Those firms target validated clinical products and diagnostic pipelines; Goodfire is an interpretability-first AI lab that uses model internals to generate hypotheses and lightweight classifiers (e.g., distilling Pleiades signals into interpretable logistic models) rather than directly shipping regulated diagnostic tests today.

competitors.name: Insitro / Recursion / Deep Genomics (AI-driven biology platforms)
competitors.similarity: Use large-scale ML and foundation-model-style approaches to discover biomarkers, predict biological responses, and accelerate translation to experiments.
competitors.how_different: Goodfire's unique emphasis is on reading and distilling what powerful foundation models have learned (interpretability → hypothesis → simple validated models) rather than primarily optimizing predictive pipelines or high-throughput wet lab automation.

competitors.name: OpenAI
competitors.similarity: Top-tier research into model capabilities and some interpretability work; broad platform and ecosystem influence.
competitors.how_different: Goodfire is niche-focused on mechanistic interpretability as a productizable capability and applying that capability to domain problems (biomedicine, production PII detection) rather than delivering general-purpose LLMs and platforms.

differentiation.primary: Turning model internals into actionable, human‑interpretable hypotheses and lightweight production probes — i.e., operationalizing interpretability end‑to‑end from research (mechanistic analysis) to deployable classifiers and guardrails.
differentiation.technical: Proprietary workflows combining sparse autoencoders (SAEs), activation probing, hierarchical attention on foundation-model embeddings, and sidecar probe architectures. Demonstrated ability to (a) train and interrogate large epigenetic foundation models (e.g., Pleiades-like embeddings), (b) discover fragmentomic biomarker signals via SAE feature attribution, and (c) distill those signals into simple, generalizable models (logistic regression) and production SAE probes that generalize from synthetic to real data.
differentiation.business: Dual-facing GTM: research-first credibility (collaborations with universities and domain teams) plus enterprise deployments (Rakuten PII probes). Focus on being an interpretability partner for regulated and safety-critical customers who need explainable, low-cost guardrails and mechanistic insight rather than opaque prediction accuracy alone.
differentiation.positioning: Positions itself between academic interpretability research labs and enterprise explainability vendors: claims academic rigor and novel methods (SAEs for semantics) while promising practical, cost-efficient production tools and domain-specific hypothesis generation (esp. in biomedicine).

secret_sauce.core_advantage: An applied interpretability platform that (1) extracts semantically coherent, sparse internal features from large models (SAEs), (2) uses those features to surface domain‑relevant signals (e.g., fragmentomics in cfDNA) that standard probes/methods miss, and (3) distills insights into lightweight, deployable classifiers and probes that generalize across cohorts and production data.
secret_sauce.defensibility: Medium: defensible because it combines specialized technical expertise in mechanistic interpretability, patented or proprietary SAE/probe training pipelines, domain partnerships and datasets (epigenetic and cfDNA data access, enterprise synthetic→production evaluation pipelines), and operational know‑how to run sidecar probes and translate internals into validated hypotheses. However, underlying methods (SAEs, probes, hierarchical attention) are academically published and could be reproduced by well-resourced competitors, so long-term defensibility depends on proprietary datasets, engineering integration, and customer relationships.
evidence: Trained SAE probes on a middle layer of Llama 3.1 8B and deployed them in production for Rakuten, with Rakuten adopting SAE probes as guardrails.
Claimed 10–500x cost savings versus LLM-as-judge setups and much better sidecar probe performance (96% vs 51%) in the Rakuten PII detection use case.

Interpretability analysis on a 7B-parameter epigenetic foundation model pretrained on ~1.9 trillion tokens uncovered fragment length as a dominant signal and enabled distillation into a logistic model that generalized to an independent cohort (AUROC 0.78; mixed model AUROC 0.84).
Published collaborative research (Goodfire + Prima Mente + Oxford) demonstrating interpretability-driven biomarker discovery and translation to simple, testable classifiers.

competitive_analysis.moat_explanation: Goodfire's moat is rooted in a specialized combo of interpretability expertise, proven production integration patterns (sidecar probes), and domain partnerships/dataset access (epigenetics cohorts, enterprise customers). These create switching costs and credibility for customers who need explainable, auditable AI in sensitive domains. However, core algorithmic ideas (SAEs, probes, hierarchical aggregators) are based on public research and can be replicated by larger incumbents or well-funded startups; sustained advantage will require continued accumulation of proprietary biological datasets, strong IP/ops around deployed probe toolchains, and deep customer integrations.

evidence_quotes: Pleiades is a 7-billion parameter epigenetic foundation model trained on human methylation and genomic sequences.
Pleiades embeddings enable accurate detection of neurodegenerative disease from blood.
To train the classifier, researchers from Prima Mente sourced data from an Oxford University study with over 30 billion cfDNA fragments across a cohort of 81 individuals.
The model was pretrained on 1.9 trillion tokens from this combined dataset. Tokens represented either individual nucleotides or the presence of a methyl group.
Two stages: pretraining on next-token prediction, followed by additional training on a reconstruction task.
interpretability platform to study the model's behavior.
distilling model performance to an interpretable classifier.

Use of BatchTopK sparse autoencoders (SAEs) as interpretability probes to decompose representation space and identify components driving classification (applied both for scientific hypothesis generation and production PII detection).

Manifold geometry analysis (projecting fragment embeddings to PCA space and measuring local curvature) to identify 'privileged' fragment lengths (e.g., 147 bp, 167 bp) where the model devotes extra representational capacity.
Two-level hierarchical attention aggregation (fragment → DMR → patient) combining foundation-model embeddings into biologically meaningful aggregation units.

Distillation of interpretability insights into a simple, fully-interpretable logistic-regression proxy trained on rank-normalized DMR-level fragmentomic metrics (with bootstrap-based feature-selection retaining features appearing in ≥90% of bootstraps).
Mitigation of batch effects by rank-normalizing features across DMRs within individuals before modeling.
Demonstrated production use-case where interpretability probes generalize from synthetic to real data and outperform LLM-as-judge baselines in cost and efficiency for PII detection.
